content.text: The Infra role in an LLMOps team is responsible for everything below the application layer: the compute fleet that runs the model, the vector and object stores that power retrieval, the observability stack that makes the system debuggable, and the CI/CD pipeline that gates every change behind an evaluation run. Where traditional infrastructure engineering focuses on stateless compute and relational data, LLMOps infrastructure has two unusual properties: the workload is
, and the "deployment unit" is not just a container but a versioned combination of model weights, prompt config, and retrieval corpus.

attrs.alt: LLMOps infrastructure stack: API gateway → LLM serving fleet → retrieval layer → observability stack → CI/CD pipeline.

content.text: The five-layer stack above defines the Infra role's ownership surface. Each layer has a distinct operational profile and failure mode, and the Infra role must be able to diagnose and remediate failures at any layer.

Every external request enters through the gateway, which enforces rate limits, authenticates callers, terminates TLS, and routes traffic to the serving fleet. In an LLMOps context the gateway also provides the canary-routing logic (splitting traffic between production and canary model versions) and the semantic cache layer (skipping the LLM entirely for near-duplicate queries).
The serving layer is where the model inference actually happens. There are two deployment modes, and teams often use both simultaneously for different use cases.

The simplest path: route requests to a third-party API (OpenAI, Anthropic, Google). The Infra role's job is to manage API keys, implement retry/fallback logic across providers, and monitor cost and rate-limit headroom.

import asyncio, time
from typing import Optional
from openai import AsyncOpenAI, RateLimitError, APITimeoutError

class LLMProviderPool:
    """Round-robin across multiple API keys to spread rate-limit load.""\"
    def __init__(self, api_keys: list[str], model: str, timeout: float = 30.0):
        self._clients = [AsyncOpenAI(api_key=k) for k in api_keys]
        self._model = model
        self._timeout = timeout
        self._idx = 0

    def _next_client(self) -> AsyncOpenAI:
        client = self._clients[self._idx % len(self._clients)]
        self._idx += 1
        return client

    async def complete(self, messages: list[dict], max_retries: int = 3) -> str:
        delay = 1.0
        for attempt in range(max_retries):
            try:
                rsp = await self._next_client().chat.completions.create(
                    model=self._model,
                    messages=messages,
                    timeout=self._timeout,
                )
                return rsp.choices[0].message.content
            except RateLimitError:
                await asyncio.sleep(delay)
                delay *= 2
            except APITimeoutError:
                if attempt == max_retries - 1:
                    raise
        raise RuntimeError("All retries exhausted")

When latency SLAs, data residency requirements, or cost at scale make managed APIs impractical, the team deploys open-weight models on GPU servers using inference servers like vLLM or Text Generation Inference (TGI). The Infra role handles GPU node provisioning, auto-scaling policies, and health management.

The retrieval layer — vector store, object store, and semantic cache — is jointly owned by the Infra and Data roles. Infra owns the infrastructure operations (provisioning, SLA, backup, scaling); Data owns the content (corpus versions, embedding model config).

: runs the approximate nearest-neighbour (ANN) search over document embeddings. Must support bulk upsert during re-index operations without downtime (hot-swap of index shards). Common choices: Weaviate, Qdrant, pgvector, Pinecone.
: holds raw corpus documents, fine-tuning datasets, model artefacts, and eval datasets. Object stores (S3/GCS/Azure Blob) provide the audit trail that lineage tracking requires.
: sits in front of the LLM and the retriever. Caching identical or near-identical prompts avoids redundant inference costs. TTL should match how often the knowledge base changes.

The observability stack is the Infra role's primary instrument for understanding system behaviour. In LLMOps it must cover not just standard infrastructure metrics (CPU, memory, network) but LLM-specific telemetry: token throughput, time-to-first-token, guardrail hit rates, and judge scores.

from opentelemetry import metrics
from opentelemetry.sdk.metrics import MeterProvider
from opentelemetry.sdk.metrics.export import PeriodicExportingMetricReader
from opentelemetry.exporter.prometheus import PrometheusMetricReader

# Wire up a Prometheus exporter; Grafana scrapes from /metrics
reader = PrometheusMetricReader()
provider = MeterProvider(metric_readers=[reader])
metrics.set_meter_provider(provider)

meter = metrics.get_meter("llmops.serving")

llm_requests = meter.create_counter(
    "llm_requests_total",
    description="Total LLM requests",
)
llm_ttft = meter.create_histogram(
    "llm_ttft_ms",
    description="Time-to-first-token in milliseconds",
    unit="ms",
)
llm_tokens = meter.create_counter(
    "llm_tokens_total",
    description="Total tokens consumed",
)

def record_request(model: str, stage: str, ttft_ms: float,
                   prompt_tokens: int, completion_tokens: int) -> None:
    attrs = {"model": model, "stage": stage}
    llm_requests.add(1, attrs)
    llm_ttft.record(ttft_ms, attrs)
    llm_tokens.add(prompt_tokens,    {**attrs, "token_type": "prompt"})
    llm_tokens.add(completion_tokens, {**attrs, "token_type": "completion"})
Every serving component should emit structured JSON logs that can be queried in a log aggregation system (Loki, CloudWatch Logs, Datadog). Include the
on every log line so all events for a single request can be correlated.
CI/CD in LLMOps differs from standard software CI/CD in one critical way: the merge gate is not just linting and unit tests but an
. A PR that changes a prompt, model config, or retrieval setting must pass an offline eval before it can merge to production.

# .github/workflows/llmops-ci.yml  (GitHub Actions example)
name: LLMOps CI

on:
  pull_request:
    paths:
      - 'prompts/**'
      - 'config/models.yaml'
      - 'retrieval/chunking_config.yaml'

jobs:
  eval-gate:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4

      - name: Run offline evaluation
        run: |
          python scripts/run_eval.py \
            --eval-dataset evals/regression_suite.jsonl \
            --config config/models.yaml \
            --min-pass-rate 0.85 \
            --output eval_results.json

      - name: Upload eval report
        uses: actions/upload-artifact@v4
        with:
          name: eval-report
          path: eval_results.json

  build-and-push:
    needs: eval-gate
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - name: Build serving container
        run: docker build -t llmops-serving:${{ github.sha }} .

      - name: Push to registry
        run: docker push ${{ secrets.REGISTRY }}/llmops-serving:${{ github.sha }}

  deploy-canary:
    needs: build-and-push
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - name: Deploy at 5 % canary
        run: |
          helm upgrade llmops-serving charts/llmops \
            --set image.tag=${{ github.sha }} \
            --set canary.enabled=true \
            --set canary.weight=5

The eval gate uses the regression suite — the fixed set of eval cases that grows with every bug fix. A PR is blocked if the pass rate on the regression suite drops below the configured threshold. This prevents prompt regressions from reaching production regardless of how innocuous the change looks in a code review.
Token costs in production can grow faster than request volume if prompt lengths are not monitored. The Infra role tracks cost per model and per feature, sets budget alerts, and enforces hard caps.
: maintain a counter of cumulative USD spend per model and stage (as shown in the Monitoring sub-subtopic). Set a Grafana alert at 80 % of monthly budget.
: if the product serves multiple customers, enforce per-tenant token budgets at the gateway layer, not the application layer — it is simpler to enforce and harder to bypass.
at the gateway to reject oversized requests before they reach the model. This is the single most effective cost control measure.
: route requests to the cheapest model tier that meets the quality requirement. Route simple classification tasks to a small model; route complex generation to a large model only when needed.
Pipeline design, eval gating, canary deploy

The Infra role in LLMOps owns the five-layer stack from API gateway through serving fleet, retrieval layer, observability, and CI/CD pipeline. Its most LLMOps-specific responsibilities are managing GPU fleets and auto-scaling for bursty inference workloads, operating the vector store for RAG, and enforcing an evaluation gate in CI/CD that blocks regressions before they reach production. Cost management — tracking token spend, enforcing prompt-length caps, and routing to the cheapest sufficient model tier — is an ongoing operational concern that distinguishes LLMOps Infra from conventional cloud infrastructure work.
